title_en: Financial Worship: Zakat and Its Detailed Rules
title_ar: Financial Worship: Zakat and Its Detailed Rules
content_en:
Ibn Qudamah devotes substantial space in Al-Mughni to zakat, recognizing it as both a pillar of worship and a complex legal institution with detailed rules that touch on questions of wealth, threshold, time, and distribution. His treatment is among the most thorough in classical fiqh literature.

The Hanbali school holds that zakat is obligatory on five categories of wealth: gold and silver (and, by extension, currencies and financial instruments), agricultural produce, livestock (camels, cattle, sheep and goats), trade goods, and extracted minerals. Each category has its own nisab (minimum threshold), haul (waiting period, where applicable), and rate.

For gold and silver, the Hanbali position sets the nisab at 20 mithqals of gold (approximately 85 grams) and 200 dirhams of silver (approximately 595 grams). The rate is 2.5% and the haul is one complete lunar year of ownership. Ibn Qudamah discusses the question of whether the gold and silver nisabs should be assessed independently or combined — the Hanbali school holds they may be combined if one metal does not individually reach its nisab and the two together represent equivalent value.

For livestock, Al-Mughni reproduces in detail the zakatable thresholds from the authentic letters (suhuf) sent by the Prophet to his governors: for camels, zakat begins at five (one sheep due); scales up progressively through twenty-four; from twenty-five, a young she-camel (bint makhad) is due; and so on through increasingly valuable animals as the herd size grows. For cattle, zakat begins at thirty; for sheep and goats, at forty. The rate and animal type due for each threshold level are specified precisely based on prophetic precedent.

For agricultural produce, the Hanbali school restricts the obligation to that which can be dried, stored, and measured — grains and dried fruits — excluding perishable items like fresh fruit and vegetables. This is a more restricted view than the Maliki school, which extends zakat to all produce. The rates (10% for rain-watered crops, 5% for irrigated crops) are established by the hadith of the Prophet (Bukhari).

For trade goods (urud al-tijarah), the Hanbali school holds that goods held with the intention of trade are subject to zakat: their value is assessed annually at market price, and if it reaches the nisab of gold or silver, 2.5% is due. Ibn Qudamah addresses the complex question of a merchant who intends to use goods both for trade and personal use — such mixed intention affects the zakat obligation.

The eight categories of zakat recipients are defined by the Quran (9:60) and Ibn Qudamah examines each in detail: the poor (fuqara') who have nothing; the needy (masakin) who have something but not enough; the zakat workers; those whose hearts are to be reconciled (mu'allafat al-qulub) — a category whose continued applicability after the early Islamic period is debated; those in bondage; debtors; those in the path of Allah (fi sabilillah) — which the Hanbali school primarily interprets as fighters in jihad; and travelers stranded without funds.

Ibn Qudamah also addresses the obligation to pay zakat promptly when it becomes due, the sin of delay without excuse, and the conditions under which an agent (wakil) may pay zakat on behalf of the owner. The depth of this discussion reflects the seriousness with which classical Hanbali scholarship treats the financial obligations of Muslims.
title_id: Ibadah Finansial: Zakat dan Ketentuan-ketentuan Rincinya
content_id:
Ibn Qudamah memberikan porsi yang sangat besar dalam Al-Mughni untuk membahas zakat, menyadari bahwa zakat adalah sekaligus rukun ibadah dan institusi hukum yang kompleks dengan ketentuan-ketentuan terperinci yang menyentuh pertanyaan-pertanyaan tentang kekayaan, nisab, haul, dan distribusi.

Pembahasan zakat dalam Al-Mughni mencakup: zakat emas dan perak, zakat perniagaan, zakat pertanian dan buah-buahan, zakat ternak (unta, sapi, kambing), zakat rikaz dan ma'din, zakat fitrah, syarat-syarat wajib zakat, nisab setiap jenis harta, delapan golongan yang berhak menerima zakat, zakat kepada kerabat, dan berbagai masalah kontemporer dalam distribusi zakat. Setiap masalah disertai dalil yang kuat dan perbandingan dengan mazhab lain.

Ibn Qudamah devotes substantial space in Al-Mughni to zakat, recognizing it as both a pillar of worship and a complex legal institution with detailed rules that touch on questions of wealth, threshold, time, and distribution. His treatment is among the most thorough in classical fiqh literature.

The Hanbali school holds that zakat is obligatory on five categories of wealth: gold and silver (and, by extension, currencies and financial instruments), agricultural produce, livestock (camels, cattle, sheep and goats), trade goods, and extracted minerals.
